---
title: "Hierarchical CNN Classification of Freshwater Zooplankton"
author: "Ethan Sansom"
date: today
format:
  pdf:
    documentclass: article
    fontsize: 11pt
    geometry: margin=1in
    number-sections: true
    colorlinks: true
    fig-pos: "H"
    tbl-cap-location: top
    fig-cap-location: bottom
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
bibliography: references.bib
---

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false

library(here)
library(jsonlite)
library(knitr)
library(dplyr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)
library(scales)
library(ggplot2)

PROJECT_DIR <- here::here()
ZOOPLANKTON_DATA_DIR <- file.path(PROJECT_DIR, "python", "zooplankton", "00_raw_data")
REPORT_DATA_DIR <- file.path(PROJECT_DIR, "resources", "00_data")
REPORT_IMAGE_DIR <- file.path(PROJECT_DIR, "resources", "00_images")

include_image <- function(file) knitr::include_graphics(file.path(REPORT_IMAGE_DIR, file))

int <- function(x) format(x, big.mark = ",")
num <- function(x) format(x, big.mark = ",", digits = 2L)
prc <- function(x) paste0(format(x * 100, digits = 2L), "%")
```

# Introduction

<!-- TODO: Squash into one paragraph and possibly shorten --->

The surveying of zooplankton populations is a key component of the Ontario Ministry of Natural Resources' (OMNR) fisheries monitoring program [@omnr_monitoring]. Zooplankton monitoring in particular
is essential for accessing the health of freshwater ecosystems. For instance,
the spread of invasive dreissenid mussels in the Laurentian Great Lakes^[The Laurentian Great Lakes will be referred to as the "Great Lakes" hereafter.] over 2000-2019 has been attributed with zooplankton biomass reductions, contributing to declining whitefish populations [@ebener2021whitefish].

Traditional zooplankton surveying relies on manual classification of sampled zooplankton
and non-zooplankton particles by trained taxonomists. More recent methods in machine learning, such as Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), offer a more efficient automated alternative. OMNR has explored several such methods, including a multi-class CNN and a hierarchical branch CNN (BCNN) for classifying images as zooplankton species or non-zooplankton objects, both achieving accuracies exceeding 95% on held-out test images [@sofia_zoop; @sofia_hiernet].

This report evaluates two alternative hierarchical CNN architectures based on the 
Local Classifier Per Parent Node (LCPN) approach [@silla2011survey] against a flat CNN baseline 
for classifying images of freshwater zooplankton and non-zooplankton debris. We examine whether a taxonomic hierarchy, and a targeted extension of it, improves classification performance over the baseline, particularly for visually similar species such as Harpacticoid and Cyclopoid copepods.

# Data and Data Processing

## Dataset

The dataset used for model training and evaluation is a non-public collection of
pre-labelled images of freshwater zooplankton and non-zooplantkon debris sampled
from Ontraio lakes including Lakes Erie, Simcoe, and Huron. The images are taken 
using FlowCam devices, which capture high resolution microscopic images of particles 
from water samples [@fluidimaging], and are provided by the OMNR.

Raw images are provided as both mosiacs, single `.tif` files containing multiple
labelled sample images, and as labelled `.tif` files of individual zooplankton
and non-zooplankton samples. The labels of the provided images are encoded in their
parent directories, e.g. images of bubbles are contained within a `bubbles/` directory. 
A Python script available from @sofia_hiernet was used to extract individual samples from 
the mosiac images.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-class-mapping
#| tbl-cap: "**Raw Image Directories and Corresponding Classes**"
#| cache: true

count_tifs <- function(dir_name) {
  path <- file.path(ZOOPLANKTON_DATA_DIR, dir_name)
  if (!dir.exists(path)) return(NA)
  length(list.files(path, pattern = "\\.tif$", ignore.case = TRUE))
}

mapping <- read_json(file.path(REPORT_DATA_DIR, "raw_to_leaf_class_map.json"))
dir_to_flat_class <- tibble(
  final_class = rep(names(mapping), lengths(mapping)),
  raw_dir = unlist(mapping, use.names = FALSE),
  image_count = sapply(raw_dir, count_tifs),
) |>
  mutate(
    final_class = final_class |> str_replace("_", " ") |> str_to_title()
  )

N_TOTAL_IMAGES <- sum(dir_to_flat_class$image_count)
N_PARTIAL_CLADOCERA <- count_tifs("cladocera")
N_PARTIAL_COPEPODA <- count_tifs("copepoda")
N_PARTIAL_IMAGES <- N_PARTIAL_CLADOCERA + N_PARTIAL_COPEPODA
N_RAW_CLASSES <- nrow(dir_to_flat_class)
N_FINAL_CLASSES <- n_distinct(dir_to_flat_class$final_class)

kable(
  dir_to_flat_class, 
  col.names = c("Class", "Raw Directory", "Image Count"),
  align = c("l", "l", "r"),
  format.args = list(big.mark = ',')
)
``` 

Following image extraction from mosaics, the raw dataset contains `r int(N_TOTAL_IMAGES)` 
fully labelled images across `r N_RAW_CLASSES` directories, which are consolidated into the `r N_FINAL_CLASSES` 
final classes used for model training (@tbl-class-mapping). The `r N_FINAL_CLASSES` final classes 
comprise the leaf nodes of a taxonomic hierarchy provided by OMNR, which organises 
freshwater zooplankton and non-zooplankton particles into a four-level tree structure 
(@fig-hierarchy).

```{r}
#| label: fig-hierarchy
#| fig-cap: "**Taxonomic Hierarchy of Freshwater Zooplankton.** Hierarchy provided 
#|   by OMNR, organising zooplankton and non-zooplankton particles into a four-level 
#|   tree structure. Leaf nodes correspond to the 11 classes used for model training and evaluation."
#| dpi: 300
#| out-width: "80%"
#| fig-align: "center"

include_image("zooplankton_hierarchy.svg")
```

An image from each of the `r N_FINAL_CLASSES` leaf classes is shown below in @fig-leaf-classes.

```{r}
#| label: fig-leaf-classes
#| fig-cap: "**Zooplankton and Non-Zooplankton Leaf Classes.**  FlowCam images 
#|   for each of the 11 leaf classes used for model training. 
#|   The first 7 classes are species of zooplankton and the last 4 are 
#|   non-zooplankton particles."
#| dpi: 300
#| out-width: "100%"
#| fig-align: "center"

knitr::include_graphics(file.path(REPORT_IMAGE_DIR, "zooplankton_leaf_classes.png"))
```

Additionally, the raw dataset contains two directories, `cladocera/` and
`copepoda/`, containing partially labelled images corresponding to parent nodes
on the hierarchy (@tbl-partial-class-mapping).

```{r}
#| label: tbl-partial-class-mapping
#| tbl-cap: "**Raw Image Directories and Corresponding Partial Classes**"
#| cache: true

dir_to_partial_class <- tibble(
    final_class = c("Cladocera", "Copepoda"),
    raw_dir = tolower(final_class),
    image_count = c(N_PARTIAL_CLADOCERA, N_PARTIAL_COPEPODA)
)
dir_to_lcpn_class <- bind_rows(dir_to_flat_class, dir_to_partial_class)

kable(
  dir_to_partial_class, 
  col.names = c("Class", "Raw Directory", "Image Count"),
  align = c("l", "l", "r"),
  format.args = list(big.mark = ',')
)
```

These images are included in the training of the hierarchical CNN models, but
are excluded from the training of the flat baseline model and are excluded from 
the validation and testing of all models.

## Preprocessing

Prior to model training and evaluation, all images are converted to greyscale on
the basis that zooplankton identification relies primarily on morphological features, e.g.
body shape, appendage structure, and silhouette, rather than colour, consistent
with how taxonomists manually classify zooplankton specimens [@leow2015copepods].
Additionally, conversion from 3 Red-Green-Blue (RGB) colour channels to a single
greyscale channel reduces image size by two-thirds, lowering memory requirements
and computational cost during training.

To improve model generalisation, three augmentations are applied randomly to
training images: a horizontal flip, vertical flip, and random 90 degree rotation.
These transformations are motivated by the lack of a canonical orientation of
particles captured by FlowCam devices. Unlike images of a human, for instance,
which typically show a person sitting or standing upright, zooplankton specimens
submitted for classification may appear in any orientation. None of these
augmentations are applied at validation or test time.

## Data Splitting

```{r}
#| label: class-counts
#| include: false

CAP <- 10000
TRAIN_FRAC <- 0.70
VAL_FRAC <- 0.10
TEST_FRAC <- 0.20

class_counts <- dir_to_lcpn_class |>
  mutate(capped_dir = pmin(image_count, CAP)) |>
  summarise(
    raw_count = sum(image_count),
    capped = sum(capped_dir),
    .by = final_class
  ) |>
  mutate(
    n_train = floor(capped * TRAIN_FRAC),
    n_val = floor(capped * VAL_FRAC),
    n_test = capped - n_train - n_val
  )
```

Following the implementation of @sofia_hiernet, each of the 11 leaf classes is
capped at 10,000 images to prevent the over-representation of majority classes,
following which the images are then divded into training, validation, and test sets using a stratified 70/10/20 split. 

Per class sample caps are neccisary given the substantial class imbalance in the raw dataset, in which *Cyclopoid* images accounting for the majority of all images (180,257). Stratification ensures that the class distribution is preserved across all three splits and, in particular,
ensures that minority classes are present in the testing dataset for evaluation purposes.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-split-counts
#| tbl-cap: "**Training, Validation, and Test Split Counts for the Flat and LCPN Models**"

flat_counts <- class_counts |>
  filter(!(final_class %in% c("Cladocera", "Copepoda"))) |>
  summarise(
    Total = sum(capped),
    Train = sum(n_train),
    Validate = sum(n_val),
    Test = sum(n_test)
  ) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), scales::comma)) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Split", values_to = "Flat")

lcpn_counts <- class_counts |>
  summarise(
    Total = sum(capped),
    Train = sum(n_train),
    Validate = sum(n_val),
    Test = sum(n_test)
  ) |>
  mutate(across(everything(), scales::comma)) |>
  pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "Split", values_to = "LCPN")

combined <- left_join(flat_counts, lcpn_counts, by = "Split")

kable(combined, align = c("l", "r", "r"))
```

The flat and hierarchical CNNs are trained on slightly different sample sizes (@tbl-split-counts).
In particular, the flat model is trained on fully labelled leaf class images only, while the 
hierarchical models additionally incorporate the `r int(N_PARTIAL_IMAGES)` partially 
labelled images assigned to the *Cladocera* and *Copepoda* nodes of class hierarchy (see @fig-hierarchy). @fig-class-distribution shows the training, validation, and testing
counts per class after sample sizes are capped.

```{r}
#| label: fig-class-distribution
#| fig-cap: "**Class Distribution After Capping.** Image counts per class
#|   after applying a 10,000 image cap. Includes the partially labelled image classes
#|   Cladocera and Copepoda. Bars are divided into training (70%), 
#|   validation (10%), and test (20%) splits. Labels within each segment show 
#|   the split count where space permits (minimum 500 images) and labels above 
#|   each bar show the total class count. See @tbl-class-distribution-full for 
#|   the complete counts. The dashed line indicates the 10,000 image cap."
#| fig-align: "center"
#| fig-width: 10
#| fig-height: 6

MIN_LABEL <- 500 # Prevents overlapping labels for test/train/validate counts

plot_data <- class_counts |>
  mutate(
    final_class = factor(
      final_class,
      levels = arrange(class_counts, desc(capped)) |> pull(final_class)
    )
  ) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(n_train, n_test, n_val),
    names_to = "split",
    values_to = "count"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    split = factor(
      split,
      levels = c("n_train", "n_test", "n_val"),
      labels = c("Train", "Test", "Validate")
    ),
    label = ifelse(count >= MIN_LABEL, scales::comma(count), NA)
  )

totals <- class_counts |>
  mutate(
    final_class = factor(
      final_class,
      levels = levels(plot_data$final_class)
    )
  )

ggplot(plot_data, aes(x = final_class, y = count, fill = split)) +
  geom_col(color = "white", lwd = 0.5, position = "stack", width = 0.75) +
  geom_text(
    aes(label = label),
    colour = "white",
    fontface = "bold",
    size = 3,
    position = position_stack(vjust = 0.5),
    na.rm = TRUE
  ) +
  geom_text(
    data = totals,
    aes(x = final_class, y = capped, label = scales::comma(capped)),
    vjust = -0.5,
    size = 3.2,
    inherit.aes = FALSE
  ) +
  geom_hline(
    yintercept = CAP,
    linetype = "dashed",
    colour = "grey40",
    linewidth = 0.5
  ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Train" = "#4C72B0", "Test" = "#55A868", "Validate" = "#DD8452")
  ) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    labels = scales::comma,
    expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))
  ) +
  labs(x = "Class", y = "Image Count", fill = "Split") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 35, hjust = 1),
    legend.position = "top",
    panel.grid.major.x = element_blank()
  )
```

Note that due to a bug in the image loading implementation, images of the *Fiber* 
class were not capped at 10,000, resulting in 11,743 images being used for training 
rather than the intended 10,000. This discrepancy did not materially affect model 
performance, and the uncapped total results in *Fiber* being only slightly 
over-represented relative to the target cap. As the classification of non-zooplankton 
objects is not a primary focus of this investigation, the models were not re-trained 
following the discovery of this bug.

\appendix

# Appendix {-}

# Additional Tables and Figures

```{r}
#| label: tbl-class-distribution-full
#| tbl-cap: "**Complete Class Distribution After Capping**"

class_counts |>
  mutate(
    final_class = as.character(final_class),
    raw_count = scales::comma(raw_count),
    capped = scales::comma(capped),
    n_train = scales::comma(n_train),
    n_val = scales::comma(n_val),
    n_test = scales::comma(n_test)
  ) |>
  rename(
    "Class" = final_class,
    "Raw Count" = raw_count,
    "Capped Count" = capped,
    "Train" = n_train,
    "Validate" = n_val,
    "Test" = n_test
  ) |>
  kable(align = c("l", "r", "r", "r", "r", "r"))
```

# References
